---
title: "Pair Programming Workshop"
subtitle: "A peer learning event for researchers who code"
---

Many researchers — especially bioinformaticians — write code as part of their work but have had little formal software training. This workshop uses pair programming as a low-barrier way to share skills, build confidence, and learn from peers.

## What is pair programming?

Two people work on one piece of code together, at one computer. One person types (the *driver*); the other thinks about direction and catches issues (the *navigator*). They switch regularly.

Unlike code review, you don't need to understand someone else's code before you arrive — you bring your own work, so you're immediately useful to your partner.

## Why it works for researchers

- **No preparation needed** — bring a script you're working on, finished or not
- **Real knowledge transfer** — you learn by doing, not watching
- **Cross-pollination** — a computational biologist and a genomics analyst may use completely different tools; working together surfaces things neither knew about
- **Low stakes** — messy, half-finished code is expected and welcome

## Event format

| Time | Activity |
|------|----------|
| 0:00 | Welcome and intro talk (~20 min) |
| 0:20 | Round 1 — pair A works on person 1's code (~25 min) |
| 0:45 | Short break and partner swap (~5 min) |
| 0:50 | Round 2 — pair B works on person 2's code (~25 min) |
| 1:15 | Optional round 3 or open hacking (~30 min) |
| 1:45 | Debrief and close (~15 min) |

Participants are paired randomly within shared language groups, and pairings rotate each round.

## Beyond the workshop

A single session is a good start — but the real value comes from making pairing a habit.

We'd encourage you to **find a buddy for every new project**, not just come to organised events. The start of a project is an especially good time to pair: when you're making design decisions, writing the first functions, or setting up your file structure, a second pair of eyes catches things that are hard to unpick later.

If you'd like to be matched with someone new — a different lab, a different tool set, a different experience level — get in touch and we'll make an introduction. Sign up below to stay in the pool.

## Further reading and watching

If you'd like to go deeper on the ideas behind pair programming:

- [**On Pair Programming**](https://martinfowler.com/articles/on-pair-programming.html) — Böckeler & Siessegger, via Martin Fowler's blog. The most thorough practical guide available: covers techniques, common objections, and remote pairing.
- [**Scientific research into pair programming**](https://tuple.app/pair-programming-guide/scientific-research-into-pair-programming) — Tuple's summary of five key empirical studies. Useful if you want to point a sceptical colleague at the evidence.
- [**Pair programming — Wikipedia**](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pair_programming) — well-sourced overview with references to the underlying academic literature.
- [**All About Pair Programming (AMA)**](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=R04qyUVo2kE) — Ben Orenstein of Tuple answers common questions. Good as a pre-event watch.


---

*These materials are part of an [SSI Fellowship](https://www.software.ac.uk/programmes/fellowship-programme) project exploring peer learning for research software developers.*
